passages.context: Old Major, the prize boar, addresses the assembled animals on the night before his death. He explains why their lives are miserable and proposes Rebellion. This is the founding speech of Animalism.
tokens: Man is the only creature that consumes without producing.
He does not give milk, he does not lay eggs,
he is too weak to pull the plough,
he cannot run fast enough to catch rabbits.
Yet he is lord of all the animals.
He sets them to work,
he gives back to them the bare minimum that will prevent them from starving,
and the rest he keeps for himself.

expertMarks.justification: This is the central charge of revolutionary socialism -- the ownership class is parasitic on producers. Orwell, writing in 1945, places into Major's mouth the rhetoric Marx and Engels use in the Communist Manifesto: workers create value, owners consume it.

'Lord' deliberately echoes the feudal and Tsarist political order Lenin's revolution overthrew. Major frames human dominance not as economics but as autocracy -- preparing the case for revolution rather than reform.

This is the doctrine of surplus value in plain English. Orwell strips Marxist economics down to a sentence a child could understand, which is how propaganda works -- and how the novel's later betrayals will work too.

Major lists female reproductive labour first -- milk and eggs are produced by bodies that are gendered female. The speech mobilises animals through their reproductive bodies but never asks who controls those bodies among the animals themselves.

'Lord' is a male-coded sovereign term -- the speech imagines the oppressor as masculine and singular. There is no analogous critique of male power inside the animal community; the future Major imagines is ungendered, which is a way of being unconsciously male.
The labour-theory of value in axiomatic form. The ruling class produces nothing; the working class produces everything. The whole power relation is encoded in two verbs -- 'consumes' and 'producing'.
Three words name the structure of capitalism -- ownership directs labour. 'He' is the active subject; 'them' are the objects of the verb. Power lives in grammar before it lives in policy.
Subsistence wage articulated explicitly. The owner returns just enough to reproduce the labour force -- no more. This is the precise relation Marx calls 'the value of labour-power'.
Surplus extraction in eight words. What is taken exceeds what is returned; the difference is the source of all wealth and all injustice.

The aphoristic opening -- short, declarative, absolute -- invites the reader to nod. Orwell makes us feel the pull of revolutionary rhetoric BEFORE the novel reveals how the same rhetoric will be twisted by Squealer in Chapter 3.

'Yet' is a hinge word -- it positions the reader in the gap between what should be (productive creatures rule) and what is (the parasite rules). The reader feels injustice as a logical contradiction before feeling it as an emotional one.

passages.reference: Chapter 3 -- Squealer on the milk and apples
passages.context: When the animals discover that the milk and windfall apples are reserved for the pigs alone, Squealer is sent to explain. This is the first major piece of pig propaganda after the Rebellion.
tokens: Comrades!
You do not imagine, I hope, that we pigs are doing this in a spirit of selfishness and privilege?
Many of us actually dislike milk and apples.
I dislike them myself.
Our sole object in taking these things is to preserve our health.
Milk and apples (this has been proved by Science, comrades) contain substances absolutely necessary to the well-being of a pig.
We pigs are brainworkers.
Day and night we are watching over your welfare.
It is for your sake that we drink that milk and eat those apples.
Do you know what would happen if we pigs failed in our duty? Jones would come back!

expertMarks.justification: 'Proved by Science' parodies the rhetoric of Soviet 'scientific socialism' -- the claim that policy follows from objective laws rather than from the interests of those who make it. Lysenko's biology, Stalin's economics, and Squealer's nutrition are the same gesture.

The threat of reaction -- that any criticism of the new regime invites the return of the old -- is the structural alibi every revolutionary government uses. Stalin invoked the Whites, Tsarists, and foreign capitalists; Squealer invokes Jones.

'Brainworkers' echoes the Soviet category of intelligentsia and the privileges granted to Party officials and managers -- those who 'thought' for the Revolution while peasants and workers laboured. Orwell names the formation of a Soviet nomenklatura as it happens.

The pig hierarchy claims intellectual labour exclusively. 'Brainworker' silently codes male -- the manual workers (Boxer, Clover) are working bodies; the brainworkers are the political class. This division mirrors the gendered intellectual hierarchy outside the novel.

Squealer pre-empts criticism by framing the audience as suspicious -- a tactic that silences dissent. The female animals (hens, cows) whose labour produces the milk and eggs Squealer is taking are absent from this address; he speaks to them but not about them.
Class privilege denies its own existence first. The very phrasing -- 'you do not imagine' -- places the suspicion in the workers' mouths so it can be denied without ever being voiced.

The naming of a new class. 'Brainworkers' replaces the old division (Man/Animal) with a new one (pig/animal). The mechanism of class formation is exposed in real time: a name, then a privilege, then a justification.

Privilege re-narrated as service. The ruling class always claims to consume on behalf of the workers. Orwell shows the rhetorical move so plainly it becomes diagnostic -- this is what extraction sounds like when it speaks for itself.

The exclamation enlists the reader as one of the addressed -- but the reader, unlike the animals, can hear what is about to follow. Orwell positions us inside the audience and slightly above it, watching the manipulation as it lands.

The personal-confession move ('I dislike them myself') is engineered to disarm. The reader spots it precisely BECAUSE Orwell has flagged it as engineering -- but Squealer's audience does not. We are positioned to see the gap between rhetoric and effect.

The exclamation, the rhetorical question, the threat -- all three at once. The reader's response is to recognise the pattern of fear-based politics. Orwell teaches us to spot the move so we will spot it elsewhere.

passages.context: After the second windmill is destroyed and food runs short, Napoleon contracts to sell four hundred eggs a week. The hens -- whose bodies produce the eggs -- refuse, in the only sustained organised resistance to Napoleon's rule in the novel.
tokens: When the hens heard this, they raised a terrible outcry.
They had been warned earlier that this sacrifice might be necessary,
but had not believed that it would really happen.
They were just getting their clutches ready for the spring sitting,
and they protested that to take the eggs away now was murder.
For the first time since the expulsion of Jones, there was something resembling a rebellion.
Led by three young Black Minorca pullets,
the hens made a determined effort to thwart Napoleon's wishes.

expertMarks.justification: The Soviet collectivisation drives of 1929 to 1933 used identical rhetoric -- 'sacrifice now for the future', 'necessary measures'. Stalin's seizure of grain from peasants killed millions; the hens' eggs are the literary equivalent. Orwell wrote this knowing the historical referent.

The word 'rebellion' was reserved for the founding event of Animal Farm -- the overthrow of Jones. Its reapplication here marks a shift: the same word now describes resistance to Napoleon. Orwell exposes that 'rebellion' is a position, not a value -- defined by who is in power.

The specific naming -- three young Black Minorca pullets -- gestures at the named opposition figures Stalin liquidated. Orwell gives them a breed and a number, dignifying their resistance against state power even as the regime's narrative will erase them.
The hens act collectively -- but the verb is not 'shrieked' or 'made a fuss'. Orwell holds the line: it is an outcry. Female collective protest is registered as serious, not hysterical.

Reproductive labour shown as planning, work, and preparation -- not as instinct. The 'clutches' and 'spring sitting' are the hens' productive cycle, the labour that has been seized. The female body is here a worksite, not a metaphor.

The hens name what is happening: not 'requisition' or 'sacrifice' (the regime's words) but 'murder'. Orwell lets the hens' political vocabulary stand -- they are theorists of their own oppression. Take the named experience seriously as analysis.

Power that pre-warns its violence trains its subjects to accept it. The 'sacrifice' belongs to the hens; the 'necessity' belongs to Napoleon. Class power lives in this distribution of agency -- who acts and who is acted upon.

The hens name the act in the language of crime, not the language of policy. This is the crucial class-power move: refusing the regime's vocabulary. Murder, not requisition. Theft, not collectivisation. The names of things are contested terrain.

'Wishes' -- a deliberately weak word. Napoleon's policy is named as personal preference, exposing that all dictatorial power is, structurally, the imposition of one will on many. The hens treat his wishes as something that can be 'thwarted' -- they refuse the legitimacy of single-person rule.

'Terrible' positions the reader: terrible to whom? To the hens, the news is terrible; to Napoleon, the outcry is terrible. The double meaning lets the reader feel both sides at once -- the violation and the regime's annoyance.

The word 'murder' in a children's-book voice is shocking. Orwell is positioning the reader: this is a fable that names killing. The reader cannot dismiss the hens' protest as melodrama because the fable form has been violated to make us notice.

'For the first time since' -- the syntactic structure positions the reader to understand this moment historically. The reader sees what Boxer and Clover do not see: a regime now produces what regimes do, an opposition. The reader's recognition is the political education.

passages.context: The pigs host a banquet for the neighbouring human farmers. The animals watch through the farmhouse window. This is the closing image of the novel.
tokens: What was it that had altered in the faces of the pigs?
Clover's old dim eyes flitted from one face to another.
Some of them had five chins, some had four, some had three.
But what was it that seemed to be melting and changing?
Twelve voices were shouting in anger,
and they were all alike.
The creatures outside looked from pig to man, and from man to pig, and from pig to man again;
but already it was impossible to say which was which.

expertMarks.justification: Twelve -- the number of disciples; or, more pointedly, the structure of a power-sharing arrangement. By 1945, Orwell had watched Stalin negotiate with Churchill and Roosevelt at Tehran and Yalta. The revolutionary vanguard has joined the world it once promised to overthrow.

The repetition -- pig/man/pig/man/pig -- mimics the rhythm of the alternating gaze and also the rhythm of historical reversal. The pigs of 1945 are Stalin's nomenklatura; the men are the Western capitalists they had once denounced. Orwell shows the convergence.

The 'already' is the historical claim: this convergence happened sooner than the animals could perceive it. Orwell's diagnosis of the Russian Revolution is exactly here -- the betrayal was complete before the betrayed had finished celebrating.

The witness to the betrayal is female. Clover -- old, dim-eyed, the work-horse mother who lost her foals to slaughter at the start -- is the moral consciousness of the novel's final scene. Orwell trusts the perception of the most exploited body. But she is given no voice, only eyes.

The bodies of the rulers are now bourgeois bodies -- fat, multi-chinned. The earlier oppressors (Jones) and the new oppressors (Napoleon) share a body type, and that body is opposed to the working bodies (Clover, Boxer) who produced it. Class is registered as flesh.
The new ruling class is fat. The fatness is the surplus value extracted from the working animals -- accumulated into bodies. Power has a physiology, and Orwell makes us see it.

The new class IS the old class. Orwell collapses the distinction Old Major drew in Chapter 1 -- between Man (parasite) and Animal (producer) -- into nothing. The lesson: revolutions that do not abolish class produce a new class indistinguishable from the old.

Class identity has become non-different -- there is no longer a meaningful category 'pig' versus 'man'. There is only ruler versus ruled. Orwell's grim conclusion: the class structure is the durable thing; the names are interchangeable.
The free indirect question is Clover's, but the reader inhabits it. We see what Clover sees -- and we have to answer. Orwell makes the reader the diagnostician of the betrayal.

The verbs -- 'melting and changing' -- describe perception failing under what it sees. The reader experiences the same perceptual instability. We do not simply learn that the pigs are now men; we feel the sentence struggle to say so.

The sentence's rhythm -- pig/man/pig/man/pig -- reads aloud as turning a head back and forth. The reader's body enacts the gaze. We are positioned outside the window with Clover, looking, looking, unable to land on either.

The closing line refuses comfort. There is no answer to the question, no return to clarity. Orwell positions the reader to leave the novel holding the same impossibility -- a literary technique that mirrors the political conclusion.
